title: Consonants: Final
description: Learn the 8 final consonant sounds in Vietnamese and how they differ from initial consonants

questions.prompt: How many distinct final consonant SOUNDS does Vietnamese have?
questions.explanation: Vietnamese has only 8 final consonant sounds: /p/, /t/, /k/, /m/, /n/, /ŋ/, /c/ (ch), /ɲ/ (nh). Far fewer than initial consonants

questions.prompt: Final 'c' and 'ch' are both pronounced as what?
questions.explanation: Both final 'c' and 'ch' become an unreleased /k/ — your tongue stops at the back but no air is released

questions.prompt: What final consonant do you hear in this word?
questions.explanation: một (one) ends with final 't' — an unreleased /t/ stop. The tongue touches behind the teeth but doesn't release

questions.prompt: What final consonant do you hear?
questions.explanation: ăn (to eat) ends with 'n' — tongue tip touches behind the upper teeth

questions.prompt: What final consonant do you hear?
questions.explanation: nóng (hot) ends with 'ng' — a velar nasal, the back of the tongue rises to the soft palate

questions.correct: Unreleased — lips close but no puff of air
options: Unreleased — lips close but no puff of air
questions.explanation: All Vietnamese stop finals (p, t, c/ch) are unreleased — you make the closure but don't release the air
cơm = cooked rice. Ends with 'm' — lips close together
mắt = eyes. Ends with final 't' — unreleased stop
đẹp = beautiful. Ends with final 'p' — lips close without release

questions.prompt: What final consonant do you hear?
questions.explanation: sách (book) ends with 'ch' — after 'a' it's written 'ch', pronounced as unreleased /k/

questions.prompt: Which finals are STOPS (block airflow completely)?
questions.explanation: p, t, c/ch are stops — they block airflow. m, n, ng/nh are nasals — air flows through the nose

questions.prompt: Which tones can appear with final stops (p, t, c/ch)?
questions.explanation: Words ending in stops (p, t, c/ch) can ONLY have sắc or nặng tone. This is a key Vietnamese phonotactic rule
mình = oneself/body. Final 'nh' appears after 'i' — palatal nasal

questions.prompt: What final consonant do you hear?
questions.explanation: tập (to practice) ends with 'p' — unreleased bilabial stop

questions.prompt: 'nh' and 'ch' as FINALS only appear after which vowels?
questions.explanation: Final 'nh'/'ch' appear after i, ê, and a. After other vowels, you use 'ng'/'c' instead
học = to study. After 'o' the final stop is written 'c' (not 'ch')

questions.prompt: What final consonant do you hear?
questions.explanation: xanh (blue/green) ends with 'nh' — after 'a', the palatal nasal

questions.correct: 'an' = tongue tip up, 'ang' = tongue back up
options: 'an' = tongue tip up, 'ang' = tongue back up
'ang' has a 'g' sound at the end
questions.explanation: Final 'n' is alveolar (tongue tip), 'ng' is velar (tongue back). Different mouth positions, very different sounds
tháng = month. After 'a', the velar nasal is written 'ng'
